**Fertilizer Use and Eutrophication**

The use of fertilizers in agriculture is very important for our food supply, but it is crucial to avoid overfertilization.
Excess phosphorus and nitrogen cannot be absorbed by plants and ends up in our rivers and lakes, and ultimately in the sea.

In these bodies of water, increased phosphorus and nitrogen inputs lead to increased phytoplankton growth, and the water becomes turbid.
In deep water, the decay of algae then leads to oxygen depletion.
The increased growth of plankton and aquatic plants and the resulting deterioration of water quality is called eutrophication. 

Further information on [nutrient input](https://www.umweltbundesamt.de/themen/wasser/fluesse/zustand/naehrstoffe)
and [eutrophication](https://www.umweltbundesamt.de/themen/wasser/gewaesser/meere/nutzung-belastungen/eutrophierung) can be found, for example, on the website of the German Federal Environment Agency.
